stem: A patient with suspected C. diff is placed in isolation. Correct precaution:
answer: Contact precautions with hand hygiene strategy appropriate for spores (per facility protocol)
rationale: C. diff requires contact/spore-focused hygiene per facility policy.
choices: Contact precautions with hand hygiene strategy appropriate for spores (per facility protocol)

stem: Which complications can occur in severe GI/hepatobiliary disease? (Select all that apply.) E. Guaranteed hypertension
rationale: Hypertension is not guaranteed; shock is common.

stem: A patient in a long-leg cast reports deep, worsening pain not relieved by opioids and pain with passive toe extension. The nurse’s priority action is:
answer: Notify the provider immediately for possible compartment syndrome
rationale: Pain out of proportion + pain with passive stretch = compartment syndrome emergency. A: Elevation may be used cautiously, but does not replace urgent escalation. C: Heat can worsen swelling. D: ROM does not treat the cause and may worsen pain.
choices: Elevate the leg above the heart and reassess in 1 hour
Notify the provider immediately for possible compartment syndrome
Apply heat to reduce muscle spasm
Encourage active ROM of toes to “work through it”

stem: Which findings support acute compartment syndrome? (Select all that apply.) E. Strong pulses rule it out
rationale: Pulses can remain present until late—pulses don’t rule it out.

stem: A patient with a femur fracture becomes acutely dyspneic, confused, and develops petechiae on the chest 24–48 hours after injury. Most likely complication:
rationale: Long-bone fracture + neuro changes + respiratory distress + petechiae = fat embolism syndrome. B: Doesn’t explain petechiae/neuro changes pattern. C: MI doesn’t cause petechiae. D: Opioids don’t cause petechial rash.

stem: After total hip arthroplasty (posterior approach), which movement must be avoided to prevent dislocation?
answer: Hip flexion >90° and adduction across midline
rationale: Posterior hip precautions: no flex >90°, no adduction, no internal rotation. B/C/D: Not hip dislocation precautions.
choices: Hip flexion >90° and adduction across midline

stem: Which patient teaching statements after posterior hip replacement are correct? (Select all that apply.) E. “Turning my toes inward is safe.”
answer: “I will use an abduction pillow when in bed if ordered.”
“I will avoid crossing my legs at the knees or ankles.”
“I will use a raised toilet seat to avoid deep hip flexion.”
rationale: Deep flexion/crossing legs/internal rotation increase dislocation risk.
choices: “I will use an abduction pillow when in bed if ordered.”
“I can bend forward to tie my shoes as long as I go slowly.”
“I will avoid crossing my legs at the knees or ankles.”
“I will use a raised toilet seat to avoid deep hip flexion.”

stem: A patient in Buck’s traction has severe heel pain. Priority nursing action:
answer: Assess skin integrity and relieve pressure; ensure heel is offloaded
rationale: Traction complications include skin breakdown and pressure injury—offload and assess first. A: Never change weights independently. C: Removing traction can worsen injury. D: Trendelenburg doesn’t address the immediate problem.
choices: Add more weight to improve alignment
Assess skin integrity and relieve pressure; ensure heel is offloaded

stem: A casted patient reports new numbness and tingling in fingers with increasing swelling. Best first response:
answer: Elevate the extremity to heart level and reassess neurovascular status promptly
rationale: Suspect neurovascular compromise; elevate to heart level and reassess promptly (then escalate if persists). B: Risk injury/skin breakdown. C: Heat increases swelling. D: New neuro symptoms are not “expected.”
choices: Elevate the extremity to heart level and reassess neurovascular status promptly
Insert fingers under cast to “stretch it out”
Apply a heating pad over the cast
Tell the patient numbness is expected for 24 hours

stem: Which assessment finding most strongly suggests impaired perfusion distal to a cast?
answer: Capillary refill >3 seconds with cool digits
rationale: Delayed refill + coolness suggests vascular compromise. A: Common and not perfusion-related. C: Can be normal post-injury. D: Nonspecific.
choices: Capillary refill >3 seconds with cool digits

stem: A patient with suspected septic arthritis has fever and a hot swollen knee. The priority next step is:
answer: Prepare for joint aspiration and start ordered antibiotics promptly after cultures
rationale: Septic joint is limb-threatening; aspiration/cultures then rapid antibiotics. A: Delay risks joint destruction. C: ROM can worsen pain/spread. D: Steroids can worsen infection.
choices: Prepare for joint aspiration and start ordered antibiotics promptly after cultures
Encourage aggressive ROM to prevent stiffness

stem: Which findings favor septic arthritis over gout? (Select all that apply.) E. Risk factors like recent bacteremia or joint surgery
answer: Severe pain with limited passive ROM
rationale: Acute monoarticular pain can be either; septic arthritis must be ruled out.
choices: Acute monoarticular pain always proves gout
Severe pain with limited passive ROM

stem: A patient with rheumatoid arthritis (RA) asks why their hands drift ulnarly over time. Best explanation:
answer: Chronic synovial inflammation leads to ligament/tendon damage and joint deformity
rationale: RA is inflammatory synovitis → pannus → destruction and deformity. A: Synovium is central in RA. C: That’s gout. D: Not required.
choices: RA primarily affects cartilage only, sparing synovium
Chronic synovial inflammation leads to ligament/tendon damage and joint deformity
RA is caused by urate crystal deposition
RA deformities occur only after fractures

stem: A patient with chronic steroid use presents with hip pain and limited internal rotation. X-ray is initially normal. Highest concern:
rationale: Steroids + hip pain → avascular necrosis can have early normal X-ray. A: More lateral hip tenderness. C: Doesn’t fit focal hip rotation limitation. D: Knee pathology.

stem: A patient has osteoporosis. Which intervention best reduces fracture risk?
answer: Weight-bearing exercise + fall prevention + appropriate calcium/vitamin D plan
rationale: Bone loading + fall prevention + nutrition/therapy reduces fractures. A: Increases deconditioning/fall risk. C: Incomplete. D: Not preventive and has risks.
choices: Weight-bearing exercise + fall prevention + appropriate calcium/vitamin D plan

stem: A patient with a suspected vertebral compression fracture reports new urinary retention and saddle anesthesia. Priority nursing action:
answer: Urgent escalation for possible cauda equina syndrome
rationale: Retention + saddle anesthesia = cauda equina emergency. A/C/D: Dangerous delay.
choices: Document and reassess after pain meds
Urgent escalation for possible cauda equina syndrome

stem: Which findings are red flags for spinal cord/cauda equina compromise? (Select all that apply.) E. Severe radicular pain with neurologic deficits
rationale: “Improved with rest” isn’t a red flag by itself.

stem: A post-op orthopedic patient suddenly has unilateral calf swelling and pain. Best immediate nursing action:
answer: Keep patient on bedrest and notify provider for DVT evaluation
rationale: Suspected DVT—avoid massage/ambulation until evaluated; escalate. A/C: Can dislodge clot/worsen. D: Unsafe.
choices: Massage the calf to reduce spasm
Keep patient on bedrest and notify provider for DVT evaluation
Encourage the patient to walk it off

stem: A patient with osteomyelitis is ordered IV antibiotics for 6 weeks. Best rationale:
answer: Bone infections are poorly vascularized; prolonged therapy is needed to eradicate infection
rationale: Bone infection often needs prolonged therapy; penetration is slower. B/C/D: Overly absolute and incorrect.
choices: Bone infections are poorly vascularized; prolonged therapy is needed to eradicate infection

stem: A patient with gout asks why colchicine helps. Best explanation:
answer: It reduces neutrophil-mediated inflammation triggered by urate crystals
rationale: Colchicine decreases inflammatory response to crystals. A: It doesn’t dissolve crystals. C: Doesn’t cure hyperuricemia. D: Different mechanism.
choices: It dissolves uric acid crystals immediately
It reduces neutrophil-mediated inflammation triggered by urate crystals
It is the same as prednisone

stem: Which medications can raise uric acid and worsen gout risk? (Select all that apply.) E. Niacin (can)
rationale: Allopurinol lowers uric acid.

stem: A patient with a dislocated shoulder reports numbness over the lateral deltoid. Which nerve injury is most likely?
rationale: Axillary nerve → deltoid weakness + sensory loss over “regimental badge” area. A/C/D: Different sensory distributions.

stem: A patient with a femoral shaft fracture is at highest immediate risk for:
rationale: Femur fractures can bleed significantly into thigh—shock risk. B/C/D: Not fracture-related priorities.

stem: Which nursing interventions reduce risk of complications after immobilization/casting? (Select all that apply.) E. Monitor for pressure areas at cast edges

rationale: Objects under cast increase skin injury/infection risk.
choices: Teach to insert objects under cast to scratch
Promote hydration and mobility as allowed to reduce constipation/DVT risk

stem: A patient trapped under debris for hours is freed and now has hypotension, hyperkalemia, and dark urine. Most likely immediate life threat:
answer: Crush syndrome with rhabdomyolysis leading to fatal dysrhythmias

choices: Acute kidney injury from myoglobin-induced tubular injury

stem: After a posterior dislocation of the hip, the nurse is most concerned about injury to which nerve?
rationale: Posterior hip dislocation threatens sciatic nerve. A/C/D: Less classic for posterior dislocation.

stem: Which assessment findings suggest sciatic nerve compromise? (Select all that apply.) E. Foot drop can occur
rationale: Knee extension/patellar reflex are more femoral.
choices: Loss of patellar reflex as hallmark

stem: A patient with a midshaft humerus fracture has wrist drop and decreased sensation on dorsum of the hand. Injured nerve:
rationale: Humeral shaft fracture → radial nerve injury. A/B/D: Different patterns.

stem: Which findings are consistent with radial nerve injury? (Select all that apply.) E. Claw hand

rationale: Palmar thumb/index = median; claw hand = ulnar.
choices: Loss of sensation on palmar thumb/index
Decreased sensation on dorsal hand (radial distribution)

stem: A patient with suspected septic arthritis has a hot swollen joint and severe pain with even minimal passive movement. Best nursing priority:

choices: Encourage aggressive ROM to prevent contracture
Maintain joint at rest; urgent escalation for aspiration/antibiotics pathway
Apply heating pad and reassess later

stem: Which findings help differentiate septic arthritis from osteoarthritis? (Select all that apply.) E. Rapid onset over hours to days
rationale: Chronic activity-related pain suggests OA.
choices: Symptoms worse with activity and improve with rest over years only

stem: A patient 2 days post total hip replacement develops sudden severe hip pain, shortened limb, and internal rotation. Most likely complication:
rationale: Sudden pain + deformity/rotation after THA suggests dislocation. B/C/D: Don’t cause limb malposition.

rationale: Day 1 mild swelling/pain is expected.

choices: Take with full glass of water and remain upright for at least 30 minutes
Crush tablet and mix with juice
Take with antacid to prevent reflux

choices: Atypical femur fractures (rare with long-term use)

rationale: Steroids/osteoporosis → vertebral compression fractures. Others less likely.

stem: Which nursing assessments are priority for neurovascular compromise in an immobilized limb? (Select all that apply.) E. Sensation and motor function

choices: Massage may dislodge a clot and cause pulmonary embolism

stem: Which conditions increase risk for DVT after orthopedic surgery? (Select all that apply.) E. Early ambulation

stem: A patient with a pelvic fracture is hypotensive. Best immediate nursing priority:

stem: A patient with cellulitis over the knee has pain, swelling, and fever. The nurse must prioritize ruling out:

rationale: Rapid progression + severe pain suggests nec fasc—time critical.
choices: Necrotizing infection if pain is out of proportion and progression is rapid

stem: Which findings are “don’t miss” for necrotizing soft tissue infection? (Select all that apply.) E. Itching that improves with moisturizer
rationale: Moisturizer response isn’t nec fasc.

stem: A patient with an external fixator has increasing drainage and pin-site redness. Best nursing action:

choices: Assess site, obtain ordered cultures if indicated, and notify provider for infection pathway
Apply occlusive dressing and do not clean

stem: Which interventions reduce risk of post-op orthopedic infection? (Select all that apply.) E. Avoiding all movement indefinitely
rationale: Total immobility increases complications.

choices: Tighten straps until numbness occurs to keep it stable
Ensure crutches fit: avoid axillary pressure; weight on hands
Place crutches high into the armpits for support
Use only one crutch to walk faster

Remove the collar briefly to “check comfort”

stem: A patient with suspected spinal cord injury develops bradycardia, hypotension, and warm dry skin below the injury. Most likely condition:

Insert objects under cast to scratch

stem: A post-op orthopedic patient on anticoagulation has increasing wound swelling, severe pain, and a tense expanding hematoma. Priority concern:

answer: Rapidly increasing swelling/tension at surgical site

rationale: Stable mild bruising is common.

Worsening pain not proportional to expected course

stem: A patient with rhabdomyolysis initially has hypocalcemia. The best explanation is:

stem: Which electrolyte patterns can occur in rhabdomyolysis? (Select all that apply.) E. Metabolic acidosis can occur

stem: A patient with a long bone fracture develops hypoxemia and confusion. You suspect fat embolism syndrome. The most appropriate immediate nursing priority is:

Apply oxygen, support ABCs, and escalate urgently
Encourage patient to ambulate to improve ventilation

stem: Which features are most consistent with fat embolism syndrome? (Select all that apply.) E. Isolated calf pain and swelling only
rationale: Calf pain/swelling suggests DVT.

stem: A patient has a knee replacement and develops calf swelling. A student nurse asks why the patient isn’t immediately put in SCDs if DVT is suspected. Best answer:
answer: Until DVT is evaluated, avoid interventions that could theoretically mobilize clot; follow facility protocol
rationale: When DVT is suspected, follow protocol; avoid actions that could mobilize clot. A/D: Too absolute. C: Incorrect.
choices: Until DVT is evaluated, avoid interventions that could theoretically mobilize clot; follow facility protocol
SCDs are contraindicated in all postop patients

stem: Which interventions reduce post-op VTE risk when appropriate? (Select all that apply.) E. Leg exercises/ankle pumps if allowed
rationale: Dehydration increases clot risk.

stem: An older adult with osteoporosis is prescribed a walker. Which finding is most urgent to address before discharge?
answer: Orthostatic hypotension and dizziness when standing
rationale: Fall risk → fracture risk; address orthostasis urgently. A/C/D: Less urgent.
choices: Orthostatic hypotension and dizziness when standing

stem: Which nursing interventions reduce fall/fracture risk in older adults with osteoporosis? (Select all that apply.) E. Home hazard assessment strategies
answer: Teach safe transfer and assistive device use
rationale: Clutter increases falls.
choices: Teach safe transfer and assistive device use

stem: A patient with suspected hip fracture is placed NPO and on bedrest pending surgery. Most important complication to prevent early:
answer: Pressure injury and pneumonia from immobility
rationale: Immobility → pressure injuries, atelectasis/pneumonia. Others not primary.
choices: Pressure injury and pneumonia from immobility

stem: Which interventions help prevent immobility complications pre-op? (Select all that apply.) E. DVT prophylaxis per protocol/orders
answer: Hydration and bowel regimen per plan
rationale: Unaffected limb movement is encouraged as allowed.
choices: Hydration and bowel regimen per plan
Encourage bedrest without any movement of unaffected limbs

stem: A patient develops sudden severe back pain, progressive leg weakness, and urinary retention after epidural anesthesia. Priority concern:
answer: Epidural hematoma compressing spinal cord/cauda equina
rationale: Back pain + neuro deficits + retention after neuraxial procedure is emergent. B/C/D: Dangerous delay.
choices: Epidural hematoma compressing spinal cord/cauda equina

stem: Which findings suggest spinal epidural hematoma and require urgent escalation? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild itching at injection site only
rationale: Itching alone is not the syndrome.

stem: After a high-speed MVC, a patient has loss of pain and temperature bilaterally at the arms with preserved light touch/proprioception. Most likely syndrome:
rationale: Central cord can affect crossing spinothalamic fibers → “cape-like” pain/temp loss, often upper extremities. A: Ipsilateral motor/proprioception loss + contralateral pain/temp below lesion. B: Loss of motor + pain/temp below; proprioception preserved. D: LMN signs + saddle anesthesia + bladder issues.

stem: Which findings are most consistent with anterior cord syndrome? (Select all that apply.) E. Often related to ischemia of anterior spinal artery
answer: Loss of motor function below lesion
Loss of pain and temperature below lesion
rationale: Anterior cord hits motor + pain/temp; spares dorsal columns.
choices: Loss of motor function below lesion
Loss of pain and temperature below lesion
Preservation of motor function with isolated sensory loss

stem: A patient has ipsilateral motor weakness and loss of vibration/proprioception with contralateral loss of pain and temperature below the lesion. Most likely:
rationale: Classic hemisection pattern = Brown-Séquard.

stem: Which findings increase concern for cauda equina syndrome? (Select all that apply.) E. Progressive lower extremity weakness
rationale: Cauda equina is LMN—hyperreflexia is not the hallmark.

stem: A patient post hip surgery suddenly becomes dyspneic, tachycardic, and anxious with pleuritic chest pain. Priority nursing action:
answer: Apply oxygen, assess ABCs, and activate rapid escalation pathway for suspected PE
rationale: Suspected PE is time-critical—support oxygenation + urgent escalation. A/C/D: Dangerous delay.
choices: Apply oxygen, assess ABCs, and activate rapid escalation pathway for suspected PE
Give PRN opioid and reassess in 2 hours

stem: Which findings support pulmonary embolism? (Select all that apply.) E. Bilateral wheezing relieved by bronchodilator always proves asthma
rationale: Wheezing can occur in PE; bronchodilator response doesn’t prove asthma.

stem: A patient with diabetes has a chronic foot ulcer. It suddenly worsens with bone tenderness and persistent drainage despite care. Highest concern:
rationale: Chronic ulcer + drainage + bone involvement suggests osteomyelitis.

stem: Which findings support osteomyelitis under a chronic ulcer? (Select all that apply.) E. Localized deep pain over bone
rationale: Moisturizer does not treat bone infection.

stem: Best rationale for prolonged IV antibiotics in osteomyelitis:
answer: Bone has relatively limited vascular supply; eradication requires extended therapy
rationale: Penetration and eradication take time; duration is long. B/C/D: Incorrect.
choices: Bone has relatively limited vascular supply; eradication requires extended therapy
Antibiotics cannot enter bone at all
Short courses always cure bone infection
Osteomyelitis is viral, so antibiotics are unnecessary

stem: Which conditions increase risk for osteomyelitis? (Select all that apply.) E. Seasonal allergies
rationale: Allergies aren’t a risk factor.

stem: A patient on chronic steroids develops new severe hip pain. X-ray is normal early. The nurse suspects:
rationale: Steroids predispose to avascular necrosis; early films can be normal. B/C/D: Less fitting.

stem: Which are complications of chronic glucocorticoid therapy? (Select all that apply.) E. Increased bone density
rationale: Steroids decrease bone density.

stem: A patient with rhabdomyolysis is improving; days later calcium levels rise above normal. Best explanation:
answer: Calcium shifts back into the bloodstream during recovery phase
rationale: Rhabdo can cause early hypocalcemia and later rebound hypercalcemia as muscle heals. B/C/D: Not necessary explanations.
choices: Calcium shifts back into the bloodstream during recovery phase

stem: Which electrolyte changes are expected in rhabdomyolysis? (Select all that apply.) E. Hypokalemia as hallmark
rationale: Hypokalemia is not typical hallmark.

stem: A patient has a fracture near the fibular head and develops foot drop. Most likely injured nerve:
rationale: Common peroneal wraps around fibular head; injury → foot drop. A/C/D: Different patterns.

stem: Which findings suggest common peroneal nerve injury? (Select all that apply.) E. Loss of patellar reflex
answer: Decreased sensation over dorsum of foot/lateral leg
rationale: Plantarflexion is tibial nerve; patellar reflex is femoral.
choices: Decreased sensation over dorsum of foot/lateral leg

stem: A patient with suspected fracture has intact pulses but worsening pain and paresthesia. Best interpretation:
answer: Compartment syndrome still possible; pulses can be present early
rationale: Pulses may remain until late—do not be falsely reassured.
choices: Compartment syndrome ruled out because pulses are present
Compartment syndrome still possible; pulses can be present early

stem: Which statements about neurovascular checks are correct? (Select all that apply.) E. Motor weakness can indicate ischemia/nerve compromise
answer: Pain with passive stretch is highly concerning
Paresthesia can be an early sign of nerve ischemia
Coolness and delayed cap refill suggest vascular compromise
rationale: Pulselessness is typically late.
choices: Pain with passive stretch is highly concerning
Paresthesia can be an early sign of nerve ischemia
Pulselessness is an early and reliable sign
Coolness and delayed cap refill suggest vascular compromise

stem: A patient with RA on immunosuppressants has a hot swollen joint and fever. The nurse’s priority is:
answer: Treat as possible septic arthritis and escalate urgently
rationale: Immunosuppressed + hot joint + fever = septic arthritis until proven otherwise.
choices: Assume it’s RA flare and give heat
Treat as possible septic arthritis and escalate urgently
Delay evaluation because fever is common in RA

stem: Which factors increase risk for septic arthritis? (Select all that apply.) E. Young healthy person has zero risk
rationale: Risk is not zero in anyone.

stem: A patient in traction reports numbness in the heel and burning pain. Most important nursing action:
answer: Assess for pressure injury and reposition/offload while maintaining alignment
rationale: Traction risks include pressure injury and nerve compression—offload and assess. A/C/D: Unsafe.
choices: Assess for pressure injury and reposition/offload while maintaining alignment

stem: Which complications are associated with prolonged immobility/traction? (Select all that apply.) E. Increased bone density
rationale: Immobility reduces bone density.

stem: A post-op joint replacement patient has increasing calf pain and swelling. The nurse’s best first action is:
answer: Keep patient at rest and notify provider for DVT evaluation per protocol
rationale: Suspected DVT—avoid massage/ambulation until evaluated.
choices: Apply SCDs tightly and ambulate immediately
Keep patient at rest and notify provider for DVT evaluation per protocol
Place heat pack and reassess tomorrow

stem: Which measures reduce risk of postoperative complications in orthopedic patients? (Select all that apply.) E. Prolonged bedrest to “protect the joint”

rationale: Prolonged bedrest increases complications.
choices: Adequate pain control to enable breathing/mobility

Reassure patient because pulses are present

stem: A post–below-knee amputation patient reports severe “crushing” pain in the absent foot. The stump is warm with good cap refill and intact pulses. Most likely:
rationale: Phantom pain = pain perceived in removed limb; perfusion is intact. A: Would show coolness, pallor, worsening perfusion signs. C: Would show systemic toxicity/worsening local findings. D: Stump findings don’t fit; DVT is different presentation.

stem: Which statements about phantom limb pain are correct? (Select all that apply.) E. Early desensitization and pain management strategies may help
answer: It is real pain perceived in the missing limb
It can be triggered by stress or stump irritation
It can coexist with residual limb pain
rationale: Phantom pain ≠ infection by default.
choices: It is real pain perceived in the missing limb
It can be triggered by stress or stump irritation
It can coexist with residual limb pain

stem: A new amputee’s stump dressing becomes saturated with bright red blood and BP drops. Priority action:
answer: Apply firm pressure/reinforce dressing and urgently escalate for hemorrhage management
rationale: Hemorrhage is immediate threat—control bleeding + escalate. A: Removing dressing can worsen bleeding. C/D: Delay.
choices: Remove the dressing to inspect the incision
Apply firm pressure/reinforce dressing and urgently escalate for hemorrhage management
Elevate limb above heart and wait

stem: Which findings suggest acute limb ischemia (in a remaining limb) requiring emergent escalation? (Select all that apply.) E. Pruritus only
rationale: Pruritus alone isn’t acute ischemia.

stem: A patient with suspected spinal cord injury initially has flaccid paralysis and absent reflexes below the lesion. This most likely represents:
rationale: Spinal shock = temporary loss of reflexes/LMN signs below injury. B: Neurogenic shock is hemodynamic (hypotension/bradycardia). C/D: Specific syndromes; not defined by reflex silence alone.

stem: Which features are consistent with spinal shock? (Select all that apply.) E. Reflexes may return later and spasticity can develop
rationale: Hypotension/bradycardia = neurogenic shock, not required for spinal shock.
choices: Hypotension and bradycardia are required to diagnose it

stem: A cervical SCI patient develops hypotension and bradycardia with warm, dry skin. Best immediate interpretation:
rationale: Loss of sympathetic tone → vasodilation + bradycardia. A: Usually tachycardia/cool clammy. C: Often tachycardia, hypoxia. D: Often tachycardia, fever/infection signs.

stem: Which nursing priorities apply in neurogenic shock? (Select all that apply.) E. Temperature regulation support (poikilothermia risk)
answer: Support hemodynamics per protocol/orders (fluids/vasopressors as ordered)
rationale: Bradycardia can occur; tachycardia isn’t hallmark.
choices: Support hemodynamics per protocol/orders (fluids/vasopressors as ordered)

stem: Post-op day 2 after knee replacement, a patient becomes acutely confused and agitated. SpO₂ is 86% on room air. Priority first action:
answer: Apply oxygen and assess ABCs; escalate for hypoxia evaluation
rationale: Hypoxia is a reversible cause; treat oxygenation first. A: Sedation can worsen respiratory failure. C: Doesn’t treat cause. D: Dangerous delay.
choices: Apply oxygen and assess ABCs; escalate for hypoxia evaluation
Restrain the patient and chart behavior
Assume delirium is normal and reorient only

stem: Which causes should be prioritized in sudden postop confusion? (Select all that apply.) E. “Normal aging” only
rationale: Do not normalize acute delirium.

stem: A post-op joint replacement patient has increasing pain after initial improvement, persistent fever, and purulent drainage. Most likely:
rationale: PJI signs include fever, drainage, worsening pain after improvement. A: Purulence is not normal. C: Wrong context. D: Not acute post-op.

rationale: Spontaneous quick resolution is not typical.
choices: Deep joint pain with reduced function
Immediate resolution within 24 hours without treatment

stem: A patient has rapidly spreading erythema, severe pain out of proportion, and hypotension. Priority concern:
rationale: Pain out of proportion + systemic toxicity = nec fasc until proven otherwise.

rationale: Moisturizer response suggests benign dermatitis.

stem: A patient with suspected open fracture is awaiting surgery. Priority nursing intervention:
answer: Cover with sterile dressing, control bleeding, immobilize, and expedite antibiotics/tetanus per protocol/orders
rationale: Open fracture = infection risk; sterile coverage + immobilize + antibiotics/tetanus pathway. A: Delays infection control. C: Increases contamination. D: Usually NPO pending surgery.
choices: Cover with sterile dressing, control bleeding, immobilize, and expedite antibiotics/tetanus per protocol/orders
Probe wound to check bone alignment
Encourage oral intake in case surgery is delayed

High-energy mechanism with skin laceration over fracture site

choices: Wound near fracture that may communicate with bone
High-energy mechanism with skin laceration over fracture site

rationale: Prevent pressure injury/nerve compression. A: Padding can help but must assess and may require cast modification. C/D: Not addressing injury risk.
choices: Offload pressure and assess skin; notify provider if cast needs modification

stem: Which interventions reduce risk of pressure injury in immobilized patients? (Select all that apply.) E. Keeping the same position to avoid pain
rationale: Immobility increases injury risk.

answer: Provide adequate pain control to enable breathing, positioning, and early mobility planning (per orders)

choices: Withhold analgesia to avoid masking symptoms
Provide adequate pain control to enable breathing, positioning, and early mobility planning (per orders)
Use only NSAIDs regardless of renal status
Sedate deeply so patient doesn’t move

stem: Which factors increase postoperative delirium risk in older ortho patients? (Select all that apply.) E. Adequate pain control always causes delirium
rationale: Adequate pain control generally reduces delirium risk; oversedation is the issue.

choices: Increasing erythema, warmth, purulent drainage, and fever

answer: Elevate briefly to reduce edema as ordered, but avoid prolonged flexion contracture positioning

rationale: Continuous pillow under knee promotes contracture.
choices: Elevate briefly to reduce edema as ordered, but avoid prolonged flexion contracture positioning
Encourage prone positioning as appropriate to prevent hip flexion contracture (for above-knee/hip risk)

choices: “Don’t put objects inside; it can injure skin and cause infection. Use cool air and prescribed itch relief methods.”

Report increasing pain, numbness, or color change immediately
Check skin at cast edges and pad rough areas per instructions

stem: Priority nursing action in rhabdomyolysis (conceptually, per orders):
answer: Aggressive IV fluids to protect kidneys; monitor electrolytes and urine output
rationale: Fluids reduce myoglobin concentration and support renal perfusion; monitor hyperK.

Encourage the patient to walk it off

stem: Which factors increase VTE risk after orthopedic surgery? (Select all that apply.) E. Regular early ambulation always eliminates VTE risk
rationale: Ambulation reduces risk but does not eliminate it.

stem: A patient receives LMWH prophylaxis and develops black tarry stools and dizziness. Best nursing action:
answer: Hold anticoagulation per protocol/orders and escalate for GI bleed evaluation
rationale: Bleeding signs on anticoagulation require urgent evaluation.
choices: Continue LMWH; stool color doesn’t matter
Hold anticoagulation per protocol/orders and escalate for GI bleed evaluation

stem: Which findings suggest major bleeding while on anticoagulants? (Select all that apply.) E. Small stable bruise
rationale: Bruising can be minor.

stem: A patient in a long-leg cast reports increasing pain and cannot dorsiflex toes. The foot is pale and cool. Priority concern:
rationale: Loss of motor function + ischemic signs = emergency.

stem: Which are key components of distal neurovascular assessment? (Select all that apply.) E. Paralysis
rationale: The “5 P’s” are classic; pulses can be late finding.

stem: A patient with suspected septic arthritis needs a joint aspiration. The nurse’s best priority is:
answer: Delay antibiotics until after aspiration if ordered/feasible per protocol while not delaying critical treatment
rationale: Cultures are important; avoid delaying life-saving therapy—coordinate per protocol.
choices: Delay antibiotics until after aspiration if ordered/feasible per protocol while not delaying critical treatment
Give oral steroids immediately without evaluation
Apply heating pad and send home

stem: Which nursing actions are appropriate in suspected septic arthritis? (Select all that apply.) E. Monitor for sepsis signs
answer: Pain control and immobilize joint as ordered
rationale: ROM is not priority in acute infection.
choices: Pain control and immobilize joint as ordered

stem: A patient with pelvic fracture is placed in a binder. Best rationale:
answer: To stabilize pelvis and reduce hemorrhage potential
rationale: Pelvic stabilization can reduce bleeding.
choices: To stabilize pelvis and reduce hemorrhage potential

stem: Which findings require urgent escalation after pelvic fracture despite stable initial imaging? (Select all that apply.) E. Stable vitals with improving pain
rationale: These suggest occult hemorrhage.

stem: A diabetic foot ulcer patient has new fever and worsening pain; the wound has foul drainage. Priority concern:
rationale: Systemic signs + foul drainage = serious infection.

stem: A patient with a cast reports numbness and tingling but pulses are present. Best nursing interpretation:
answer: Early neurovascular compromise can occur with pulses intact—escalate based on full neurovascular assessment
rationale: Pulses can remain present early; neuro findings matter.
choices: Early neurovascular compromise can occur with pulses intact—escalate based on full neurovascular assessment

stem: A patient from an enclosed-space fire has headache, confusion, and cherry-red appearing skin (may be absent). SpO₂ reads 99%. Best interpretation:
answer: Carbon monoxide poisoning can show normal pulse oximetry; treat as toxic exposure
rationale: Pulse ox cannot distinguish oxyhemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin.
choices: Carbon monoxide poisoning can show normal pulse oximetry; treat as toxic exposure

stem: Which findings raise concern for carbon monoxide poisoning? (Select all that apply.) E. Unilateral leg swelling
rationale: Leg swelling is unrelated.

stem: A fire victim rapidly collapses with severe lactic acidosis and cardiovascular instability. Highest concern in addition to CO:
rationale: Smoke inhalation can cause cyanide toxicity → profound lactic acidosis and shock.

stem: Which clinical clues support cyanide toxicity in smoke inhalation? (Select all that apply.) E. Neurologic deterioration (seizures/coma)
rationale: Cyanide toxicity is acute, not weeks-long.

stem: A major burn patient develops rising airway pressures, worsening hypoxemia, and hypotension on the ventilator. Priority concern:
rationale: Vent high pressures + shock + hypoxemia suggests pneumothorax—emergent.

stem: Which findings suggest tension pneumothorax in a ventilated burn patient? (Select all that apply.) E. Improved BP and oxygenation
rationale: Improvement is not consistent.

stem: A patient starts a new sulfa antibiotic and develops fever, targetoid lesions, and painful mucosal erosions. Priority diagnosis:
rationale: Mucosal involvement + systemic symptoms after medication is SJS/TEN until proven otherwise.

stem: Which findings support SJS/TEN? (Select all that apply.) E. Recent high-risk medication exposure
rationale: Benign itchy rash without mucosa is less consistent.
choices: Localized itchy rash only with no mucosa

stem: Priority nursing action when SJS/TEN is suspected:
answer: Stop suspected agent and escalate emergently; supportive care like burn management may be needed
rationale: Discontinuation + urgent care is essential; can be life-threatening.
choices: Continue the offending medication to “finish the course”
Stop suspected agent and escalate emergently; supportive care like burn management may be needed

stem: A patient has isolated lip swelling after starting an ACE inhibitor; no hives but voice feels “thick.” Priority concern:
rationale: Angioedema can progress to airway obstruction; may lack urticaria.

stem: Which findings suggest angioedema requiring airway vigilance? (Select all that apply.) E. Drooling/dysphagia
rationale: Hypotension/wheezing suggests anaphylaxis, but angioedema can be isolated.

stem: A patient with cellulitis develops fever, tachycardia, and rapidly spreading erythema despite antibiotics. Priority concern:
answer: Progression to sepsis or deeper infection (nec fasc)
rationale: Rapid spread and systemic signs = escalation.
choices: Progression to sepsis or deeper infection (nec fasc)

stem: Which features suggest cellulitis may be complicated (deeper infection or systemic spread)? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild localized redness with improvement
rationale: Improvement is reassuring.

stem: Red streaking from a hand wound toward the axilla with tender nodes most consistent with:
rationale: Streaking erythema along lymphatics with nodes = lymphangitis.

stem: Which findings support lymphangitis? (Select all that apply.) E. Often follows skin infection/injury
answer: Linear red streaks proximal to wound
rationale: Sacral erythema suggests pressure injury, not lymphangitis.
choices: Linear red streaks proximal to wound

stem: A neutropenic patient has minimal redness at a port site but new fever and chills. Best nursing interpretation:
answer: Infection can be subtle in neutropenia—treat as emergency and escalate
rationale: Immunosuppressed patients may lack robust local signs.
choices: Infection unlikely because skin looks normal
Infection can be subtle in neutropenia—treat as emergency and escalate

stem: Which nursing actions are priorities for suspected central line infection in an immunocompromised patient? (Select all that apply.) E. Maintain aseptic technique during all access
answer: Obtain cultures per protocol (including line/peripheral if ordered)
Start ordered broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly after cultures per protocol
rationale: Flushing does not clear infection and may seed bloodstream.
choices: Obtain cultures per protocol (including line/peripheral if ordered)
Start ordered broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly after cultures per protocol

stem: A surgical incision is warm with increasing pain, purulent drainage, and wound edges separating. Priority action:
answer: Notify provider and implement wound infection/dehiscence protocol; monitor for sepsis
rationale: Purulence + dehiscence indicates serious wound complication.
choices: Notify provider and implement wound infection/dehiscence protocol; monitor for sepsis

stem: Which findings indicate wound infection rather than normal inflammation? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild expected tenderness that steadily improves
answer: Increasing erythema and pain after initial improvement
rationale: Improving mild tenderness is normal.
choices: Increasing erythema and pain after initial improvement

stem: A patient with extensive burns suddenly has decreased urine output and rising creatinine despite fluids. Priority concern:

rationale: Burn shock and rhabdo can contribute—monitor perfusion and complications.
choices: AKI from hypoperfusion or pigment injury; requires urgent reassessment of resuscitation and complications

stem: Which complications are common after major burns? (Select all that apply.) E. Guaranteed hypertension
rationale: Hypertension is not guaranteed; hypotension is common early.

answer: Local redness may be minimal despite severe infection
Fever may be the only sign

choices: Local redness may be minimal despite severe infection
Fever may be the only sign

stem: A patient has sudden painless monocular vision loss described as “a shade came down,” and the retina appears pale with a cherry-red spot. Most likely:
rationale: Pale retina + cherry-red spot is classic CRAO—true ocular emergency.

stem: Which findings support CRAO? (Select all that apply.) E. Vascular risk factors (AFib, carotid disease)
rationale: Pain + halos = angle-closure glaucoma.
choices: Severe eye pain with halos and vomiting

stem: An older adult has new unilateral headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, and blurred vision. Priority concern:
rationale: Temporal arteritis threatens permanent vision loss—treat urgently.

stem: Which findings support temporal arteritis? (Select all that apply.) E. Itching only without pain
rationale: Itching alone is not typical.

stem: Priority action when temporal arteritis is suspected:
answer: Start high-dose steroids per protocol/orders urgently to protect vision
rationale: Do not delay steroids—vision loss can be irreversible.
choices: Delay treatment until biopsy results return
Start high-dose steroids per protocol/orders urgently to protect vision

stem: A patient has painful eye movement and decreased vision after a viral illness; color vision is reduced. Most likely:
rationale: Optic neuritis often causes pain with eye movement and color desaturation.

stem: Which findings support optic neuritis? (Select all that apply.) E. Afferent pupillary defect (can occur)
rationale: Cherry-red spot pattern is CRAO.
choices: Sudden painless loss with pale retina/cherry-red spot

stem: A patient has acute vertigo, vomiting, and gait instability. The nurse’s biggest “don’t miss” concern is:
rationale: Acute vestibular syndrome can be stroke—especially with neuro signs.

stem: Which findings suggest vertigo may be central (stroke) rather than peripheral? (Select all that apply.) E. New headache/neck pain (possible dissection)
answer: New focal neurologic deficits (dysarthria, limb ataxia)
Severe truncal ataxia/inability to walk unassisted
rationale: Brief positional vertigo suggests BPPV (peripheral).
choices: New focal neurologic deficits (dysarthria, limb ataxia)
Severe truncal ataxia/inability to walk unassisted

stem: A diabetic older adult with severe ear pain has granulation tissue in ear canal and cranial nerve deficits. Most concerning diagnosis:
rationale: Immunocompromised + severe otalgia + granulation tissue = malignant OE with skull base osteomyelitis risk.

stem: Which findings support malignant otitis externa? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild itch only, resolves in a day
rationale: Mild itch only is uncomplicated.

stem: A patient after cataract surgery has sudden severe eye pain, decreased vision, and nausea. Priority concern:
answer: Endophthalmitis or acute secondary glaucoma—vision-threatening emergency
rationale: Severe pain + vision loss post-op is never “normal.”
choices: Endophthalmitis or acute secondary glaucoma—vision-threatening emergency

stem: Which post-op eye symptoms require urgent evaluation? (Select all that apply.) E. Nausea/vomiting with eye pain
rationale: Mild scratchiness can be expected; severe symptoms are red flags.

stem: A patient has flashes and floaters after trauma plus decreased vision. Best action:
answer: Urgent ophthalmology evaluation for retinal tear/detachment
rationale: Trauma increases risk of tear/detachment—urgent.
choices: Urgent ophthalmology evaluation for retinal tear/detachment

stem: Which patients are higher risk for retinal tear/detachment? (Select all that apply.) E. Stable floaters unchanged for years only
rationale: Long-standing stable floaters are less concerning.

stem: A patient with ear infection has postauricular swelling and the ear protrudes forward. Priority concern:
rationale: Postauricular swelling + protruding ear suggests mastoiditis—complication risk.

stem: Which findings suggest mastoiditis complications requiring escalation? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild itch only
rationale: Mild itch suggests external canal irritation.

stem: A patient has painful red eye, photophobia, and contact lens use. Priority concern:
rationale: Contact lenses → high risk for corneal infection and rapid damage.

stem: Which actions are appropriate if infectious keratitis is suspected? (Select all that apply.) E. Assess visual acuity and pain severity
rationale: Delays can threaten vision.
choices: Delay care to see if it improves

stem: A patient has sudden painless monocular vision loss and a history of atrial fibrillation. Priority nursing implication:
answer: Emergency ocular ischemia—activate urgent evaluation pathway
rationale: Embolic ocular events are emergencies.
choices: Emergency ocular ischemia—activate urgent evaluation pathway

stem: Which “vision loss” presentations are true emergencies? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild dry eye discomfort only
rationale: Dry eye is not emergent.

stem: A patient with acute vestibular syndrome has vertigo and vomiting plus new dysarthria. Best next action:
rationale: Neuro deficit + acute vertigo = possible posterior stroke.

stem: Which symptoms suggest vertigo is not benign peripheral disease? (Select all that apply.) E. Brief vertigo only when rolling in bed with no neuro signs
rationale: Brief positional vertigo is often peripheral (BPPV).

stem: A patient has rapidly worsening erythema, severe pain out of proportion, fever, and dusky skin with bullae. Priority concern:
rationale: Pain out of proportion + rapid progression + bullae/systemic toxicity = nec fasc until proven otherwise.

stem: Which findings suggest necrotizing fasciitis rather than uncomplicated cellulitis? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild itching only with stable vitals
answer: Severe pain out of proportion to exam
rationale: Mild itching alone suggests dermatitis.
choices: Severe pain out of proportion to exam

stem: First priority nursing action for suspected necrotizing fasciitis:
answer: Activate emergent escalation for surgical evaluation and broad-spectrum antibiotics per protocol/orders
rationale: Definitive management is urgent surgical debridement + antibiotics.
choices: Activate emergent escalation for surgical evaluation and broad-spectrum antibiotics per protocol/orders
Reassure and schedule clinic visit next week

stem: A patient has a fluctuant, tender, warm lesion with a central “point.” Most likely:
rationale: Fluctuance suggests pus collection.

stem: Which findings support abscess rather than cellulitis alone? (Select all that apply.) E. Pain localized to a central area
rationale: Diffuse erythema without collection fits cellulitis.

stem: A patient has unilateral calf swelling, warmth, and pain after surgery. Skin is not significantly erythematous. Priority concern:
rationale: Post-op unilateral swelling/pain suggests DVT—potential PE risk.

stem: Which findings support DVT over cellulitis? (Select all that apply.) E. Sudden dyspnea/pleuritic pain (suggests PE from DVT)
answer: Minimal skin erythema with deep tenderness
rationale: Necrosis/toxicity suggests nec fasc, not typical DVT.
choices: Minimal skin erythema with deep tenderness
High fever with rapidly spreading skin necrosis

stem: A burn patient has facial burns, singed nasal hairs, and hoarseness. Priority nursing concern:
answer: Inhalation injury with impending airway compromise
rationale: Airway edema can progress rapidly—secure airway early.
choices: Inhalation injury with impending airway compromise

stem: Which findings suggest inhalation injury? (Select all that apply.) E. Localized blister on forearm only
rationale: Isolated arm burn doesn’t suggest inhalation injury.

stem: A major burn patient becomes hypotensive with tachycardia in the first hours. Most likely cause:
answer: Hypovolemic shock from capillary leak/fluid shifts
rationale: Early burn shock is primarily fluid loss into tissues.
choices: Hypovolemic shock from capillary leak/fluid shifts

stem: Priority early burn management concepts include: (Select all that apply.) E. Temperature control/prevent hypothermia
answer: Large-bore IV access and fluid resuscitation per protocol
rationale: Tight wraps can worsen ischemia/compartment issues.
choices: Large-bore IV access and fluid resuscitation per protocol
Prophylactic tight circumferential bandages to reduce swelling

stem: A circumferential full-thickness arm burn develops increasing pain, pallor, and decreased pulses distal to burn. Priority intervention:
rationale: Circumferential burns can act like a tourniquet; escharotomy may be needed.
choices: Apply tight compression to reduce swelling

stem: Which findings suggest vascular compromise from circumferential burns? (Select all that apply.) E. Warm pink fingers with strong pulses
rationale: Warm/pink with strong pulses is reassuring.

stem: A pressure injury is intact skin with non-blanchable erythema over a bony prominence. Stage:
rationale: Non-blanchable erythema with intact skin = Stage 1.

stem: Which are characteristics of a Stage 2 pressure injury? (Select all that apply.) E. Pink/red moist wound bed
answer: Shallow open ulcer or serum-filled blister
rationale: Exposed bone is Stage 4; slough obscuring base = unstageable.
choices: Shallow open ulcer or serum-filled blister

stem: A wound has full-thickness loss, adipose visible, but no exposed bone/tendon. Stage:
rationale: Stage 3 exposes subcutaneous fat; Stage 4 exposes bone/tendon/muscle.

stem: Which findings indicate an unstageable pressure injury? (Select all that apply.) E. Always Stage 2
answer: Full-thickness skin/tissue loss with slough or eschar obscuring base
Cannot determine depth until slough/eschar removed
rationale: Unstageable cannot be staged until base is visible; not “always Stage 2.”
choices: Full-thickness skin/tissue loss with slough or eschar obscuring base
Cannot determine depth until slough/eschar removed

stem: A patient develops hives, wheezing, hypotension, and lip swelling after antibiotic. Priority medication class:
rationale: Anaphylaxis = epinephrine first-line; airway/shock risk.

stem: Which findings support anaphylaxis? (Select all that apply.) E. Rapid onset after exposure
rationale: Localized rash alone may be mild reaction.
choices: Isolated localized rash without systemic symptoms only

stem: A patient with neutropenia has temp 38.3°C (101°F) but minimal symptoms. Priority action:
answer: Treat as neutropenic fever emergency and escalate for cultures and antibiotics per protocol
rationale: Neutropenic patients may not mount strong symptoms; fever can be only sign of severe infection.
choices: Treat as neutropenic fever emergency and escalate for cultures and antibiotics per protocol

stem: Which are key nursing priorities in neutropenic fever? (Select all that apply.) E. Monitor for sepsis signs even if subtle
answer: Rapid escalation and start ordered broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly after cultures per protocol
Strict hand hygiene and infection prevention
rationale: Live vaccines are generally avoided in immunocompromised states.
choices: Rapid escalation and start ordered broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly after cultures per protocol
Strict hand hygiene and infection prevention

stem: A patient with recurrent cellulitis asks how to reduce risk. Best advice:
answer: Skin care: treat tinea, moisturize, manage edema, protect breaks, hygiene
rationale: Barrier protection and edema control reduce recurrence.
choices: Skin care: treat tinea, moisturize, manage edema, protect breaks, hygiene

stem: Which interventions help prevent pressure injuries? (Select all that apply.) E. Massage reddened bony prominences vigorously
rationale: Massaging reddened areas can worsen tissue injury.

stem: A type 1 diabetic has abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and fruity breath. Most likely:
rationale: Classic DKA signs: ketones + acidosis + Kussmaul respirations.

stem: Which findings are more consistent with DKA than HHS? (Select all that apply.) E. Fruity breath odor
rationale: HHS = severe hyperosmolarity with little/no ketosis.
choices: Profound hyperosmolarity with minimal acidosis always

stem: A patient with type 2 diabetes is confused and severely dehydrated; glucose 980 with minimal ketones. Most likely:
rationale: Severe hyperglycemia + hyperosmolar dehydration + minimal ketosis = HHS.

stem: Which findings support HHS? (Select all that apply.) E. High serum osmolality (concept)
rationale: Kussmaul acidosis pattern is more DKA.
choices: Significant metabolic acidosis with Kussmaul respirations as hallmark

stem: In DKA, which intervention is typically the first priority (after ABCs)?
answer: Rapid isotonic fluid resuscitation per protocol
rationale: Fluids restore perfusion and begin lowering glucose; insulin follows with careful K monitoring.
choices: Rapid isotonic fluid resuscitation per protocol

stem: Key DKA management concepts (per protocol/orders) include: (Select all that apply.) E. Stop monitoring once glucose improves
answer: Insulin therapy after initial volume resuscitation
Monitor and replace potassium as needed
rationale: DKA requires continued monitoring until anion gap closes.
choices: Insulin therapy after initial volume resuscitation
Monitor and replace potassium as needed

stem: A DKA patient’s potassium is 3.1 mEq/L. Best next action conceptually:
rationale: Insulin drives K into cells → can cause fatal hypokalemia if K is already low.

stem: Which statements about potassium in DKA are correct? (Select all that apply.) E. Continuous ECG/lab monitoring is important
answer: Serum K may be normal/high despite total body K depletion
Insulin therapy can rapidly lower serum K
Hypokalemia must be corrected before starting insulin (per protocol)
rationale: Potassium shifts dynamically during therapy.
choices: Serum K may be normal/high despite total body K depletion
Insulin therapy can rapidly lower serum K
Hypokalemia must be corrected before starting insulin (per protocol)

stem: A patient on chronic steroids has vomiting, severe weakness, hypotension, and hyponatremia after sudden steroid discontinuation. Most likely:
rationale: Adrenal crisis = shock + electrolyte abnormalities after steroid withdrawal/stress.

stem: Which findings support adrenal crisis? (Select all that apply.) E. Hypertension with bradycardia as hallmark
rationale: Hypertension/bradycardia is not typical.

stem: Priority management in adrenal crisis (conceptually, per orders) is:
answer: IV fluids and stress-dose corticosteroids promptly
rationale: Treat immediately—do not wait for labs.
choices: IV fluids and stress-dose corticosteroids promptly

stem: A hyperthyroid patient has fever, severe tachycardia, agitation, and diarrhea. Most likely:
rationale: Thyroid storm = life-threatening hypermetabolic state.

stem: Which findings support thyroid storm? (Select all that apply.) E. GI symptoms (diarrhea/vomiting)
rationale: Bradycardia/hypothermia suggests myxedema coma.

stem: A hypothyroid patient is found hypothermic, bradycardic, and obtunded after exposure to cold. Most likely:
rationale: Myxedema coma = severe hypothyroidism with hypothermia/bradycardia/AMS.

stem: Which findings support myxedema coma? (Select all that apply.) E. Altered mental status
rationale: Agitation/diarrhea align with thyroid storm.

rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia is an emergency.
choices: Severe hyponatremia with cerebral edema risk

stem: Which findings support SIADH rather than diabetes insipidus? (Select all that apply.) E. Weight gain without edema can occur
rationale: DI causes high output dilute urine and hypernatremia.

stem: A patient has polyuria, intense thirst, hypernatremia, and very dilute urine. Most likely:
rationale: DI = inability to concentrate urine → free water loss.

stem: Which findings support diabetes insipidus? (Select all that apply.) E. Dehydration signs
rationale: DI typically has high serum osmolality, not low.

stem: A diabetic patient with slurred speech and unilateral weakness arrives to ED. Before activating stroke thrombolytics pathway, the nurse should first:
rationale: Hypoglycemia can mimic stroke—rapid glucose check is essential.
choices: Delay assessment until CT is done

stem: Which findings are consistent with hypoglycemia? (Select all that apply.) E. Seizures in severe cases
rationale: Bradycardia/hypothermia is not the hallmark pattern.

stem: A patient takes rapid-acting insulin but then misses their meal. Highest risk:
rationale: Rapid-acting insulin without carbohydrate intake → hypoglycemia.

choices: Verify glucose and ensure meal availability when appropriate

stem: A patient with type 2 diabetes has glucose 780, profound dehydration, and mild ketones with mild acidosis. Best interpretation:
answer: HHS with possible DKA overlap can occur—treat as hyperglycemic crisis per protocol
rationale: DKA/HHS can overlap; management still follows hyperglycemic crisis protocols.
choices: HHS with possible DKA overlap can occur—treat as hyperglycemic crisis per protocol

stem: Which findings suggest HHS-DKA overlap? (Select all that apply.) E. Severe dehydration signs
answer: Very high glucose with hyperosmolar dehydration
Some degree of metabolic acidosis/an anion gap

stem: During DKA treatment, the nurse notes glucose is improving but anion gap remains elevated. Best nursing interpretation:
answer: Ketosis/acidosis may still be present; continue protocol until anion gap closes and patient stable
rationale: DKA resolution requires closure of anion gap and correction of acidosis, not glucose alone.
choices: Ketosis/acidosis may still be present; continue protocol until anion gap closes and patient stable

stem: Which are typical criteria/concepts used to determine DKA resolution (protocol-dependent)? (Select all that apply.) E. Mental status/clinical stability improves
answer: Patient able to tolerate PO and transition plan in place
rationale: Glucose alone is not enough.
choices: Patient able to tolerate PO and transition plan in place

stem: When an insulin drip is used for DKA, why is dextrose sometimes added to IV fluids before stopping insulin?
answer: To allow continued insulin to clear ketones while preventing hypoglycemia
rationale: Insulin must continue until ketosis resolves; dextrose prevents low glucose.
choices: To allow continued insulin to clear ketones while preventing hypoglycemia
To stop anion gap from closing

stem: Which are correct principles during insulin infusion for DKA? (Select all that apply.) E. Stop insulin as soon as glucose is normal regardless of anion gap
answer: Continue insulin until ketosis/acidosis resolves, not just until glucose improves
Add dextrose to fluids when glucose reaches protocol threshold to continue insulin safely
rationale: Stopping early can rebound ketosis.
choices: Continue insulin until ketosis/acidosis resolves, not just until glucose improves
Add dextrose to fluids when glucose reaches protocol threshold to continue insulin safely

rationale: Risk is with overly rapid correction, not slow.
choices: Very slow correction always causes it

choices: Weight gain without peripheral edema (can occur)

choices: Reassess vitals, glucose, electrolytes, and neuro status

stem: A patient is hypotensive after GI bleed and now has oliguria. Most likely AKI category:
rationale: Low perfusion (shock/volume loss) → prerenal AKI.

stem: Which findings support prerenal AKI (conceptually)? (Select all that apply.) E. Rising creatinine after nephrotoxin exposure only

rationale: Obstruction = postrenal; nephrotoxins often intrinsic.
choices: History of volume loss or poor perfusion

stem: A patient has sudden anuria and suprapubic fullness after surgery. Priority action:

rationale: Postop urinary retention is common and treatable; evaluate quickly.
choices: Assess for urinary retention and bladder distention; bladder scan and notify provider per protocol

stem: A patient on aminoglycosides develops rising creatinine and muddy brown casts. Most likely AKI type:
rationale: Nephrotoxin + muddy brown casts → ATN.

stem: Which exposures increase risk for intrinsic AKI? (Select all that apply.) E. Prolonged hypotension causing ischemic ATN
rationale: Mild dehydration alone is typically prerenal.

stem: A renal failure patient has peaked T waves and QRS widening. Priority nursing concern:
rationale: ECG changes signal malignant arrhythmia risk.

stem: Which interventions are used in emergent hyperkalemia management (conceptually, per protocol/orders)? (Select all that apply.) E. Potassium supplements
rationale: Supplements worsen hyperkalemia.

stem: A patient with CKD becomes confused and has pericardial friction rub and chest pain. Highest concern:
rationale: Uremia can cause pericarditis; potentially life-threatening.

stem: Which findings support uremic syndrome? (Select all that apply.) E. Improved energy and appetite
rationale: Uremia causes systemic illness, not improvement.

stem: A hemodialysis patient becomes hypotensive and cramps during treatment. Best explanation:
rationale: Rapid ultrafiltration can cause hypotension/cramps.

stem: Which interventions can help manage intradialytic hypotension (conceptually, per protocol/orders)? (Select all that apply.) E. Reassess dry weight/volume targets
answer: Trend vital signs closely and stop/slow ultrafiltration as directed
rationale: Diuretics don’t treat intradialytic hypotension.
choices: Trend vital signs closely and stop/slow ultrafiltration as directed

stem: A dialysis patient with an AV fistula has sudden swelling and pain in the access arm with absent thrill/bruit. Priority concern:
rationale: Loss of thrill/bruit suggests access failure—urgent.

stem: Which AV fistula care principles are correct? (Select all that apply.) E. Monitor for signs of infection
answer: Avoid BP measurements/venipuncture on access arm
Report sudden loss of thrill/bruit immediately
rationale: Tight tourniquets can compromise access.
choices: Avoid BP measurements/venipuncture on access arm
Report sudden loss of thrill/bruit immediately
Apply tight tourniquet for long periods

stem: A patient with pyelonephritis risk has fever, flank pain, and nausea. Priority action:

rationale: Pyelo can progress to sepsis—early antibiotics matter.
choices: Escalate for infection workup and start ordered antibiotics promptly

stem: Which findings support pyelonephritis rather than simple cystitis? (Select all that apply.) E. Signs of sepsis (hypotension, confusion) in severe cases
rationale: Dysuria alone suggests lower UTI.
choices: Dysuria only with no systemic symptoms

stem: An older adult with UTI becomes acutely confused and hypotensive. Priority concern:
rationale: AMS + hypotension + infection signs = sepsis until proven otherwise.

stem: Which findings indicate urosepsis requiring urgent escalation? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild dysuria with normal vitals
rationale: Mild dysuria with stable vitals is not urosepsis.

stem: A patient with urinary retention has a Foley inserted and suddenly develops gross hematuria and abdominal discomfort. Best interpretation:

rationale: Rapid decompression/trauma can cause hematuria; needs evaluation.
choices: Possible bladder decompression complications/trauma; monitor and escalate per protocol

stem: Which factors increase risk of contrast-associated kidney injury? (Select all that apply.) E. Young healthy patient with normal kidneys always
rationale: Healthy kidneys are lower risk.

rationale: Low pH + low HCO₃ = metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (low CO₂).

stem: Which clinical findings are consistent with volume overload in renal failure? (Select all that apply.) E. Dry mucous membranes and sunken eyes
rationale: Dry mucous membranes suggest dehydration.

stem: A patient with suspected AKI asks why urine output is watched so closely. Best response:

stem: Urinalysis shows hematuria with RBC casts, hypertension, and periorbital edema. Most consistent with:
rationale: Hematuria + RBC casts + HTN suggests inflammatory glomerular process (nephritic).

rationale: RBC casts point to nephritic syndrome.

stem: A patient with nephrotic syndrome develops sudden dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Priority concern:

stem: Which findings support dialysis disequilibrium syndrome? (Select all that apply.) E. Immediate symptom relief after large free-water intake

rationale: Withholding fluids can cause shock.
choices: Replace fluids per protocol/orders to avoid hypovolemia
Withhold all fluids to stop diuresis

stem: A patient has dysuria and urinary frequency without fever or flank pain. Most likely diagnosis:
rationale: Local urinary symptoms without systemic signs suggests lower UTI.

stem: Which findings shift concern from cystitis to pyelonephritis/urosepsis? (Select all that apply.) E. Mild dysuria only
rationale: Mild dysuria alone is lower UTI.

stem: Best strategy to reduce CAUTI risk in an inpatient with a Foley catheter:

rationale: Shortest duration + closed system = strongest prevention.
choices: Remove catheter as soon as no longer indicated and maintain closed sterile system

stem: Which practices reduce CAUTI risk? (Select all that apply.) E. Daily assessment of necessity and prompt removal when not needed
rationale: Routine irrigation increases infection risk.

stem: ECG shows flattened T waves and prominent U waves. Most likely electrolyte abnormality:

stem: Which findings are consistent with hypokalemia? (Select all that apply.) E. Ileus/constipation can occur

rationale: Hypocalcemia can cause neuromuscular irritability and QT prolongation.

stem: Which findings suggest hypocalcemia? (Select all that apply.) E. Positive Trousseau/Chvostek (concept)
rationale: Shortened QT suggests hypercalcemia.

stem: A renal failure patient has phosphate elevated and calcium low. Best concept:

It proves hyperparathyroidism from tumor only

choices: Monitor BP, renal function, and fluid balance closely; escalate worsening signs

stem: Which are concerning signs of worsening renal function that require escalation? (Select all that apply.) E. Stable vitals and improving urine output
rationale: Improving output is reassuring.

choices: Screen for CKD/diabetes/volume depletion and ensure prophylaxis plan per protocol if indicated
